narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and an impressive LSAT score of 38.50, you've clearly put in the work to build a strong academic foundation, coming from an upper-middle-class family background and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on their first try. For you, it estimated a 64% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that led to your application being rejected—it's a tough call designed to prioritize those with the highest predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, which is below the typical 5.53 average and signaled a dip in performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 6th, slightly above the average of 5.71, also contributed somewhat to concerns about consistency, though less strongly. On a positive note, your standout LSAT score pushed meaningfully in favor of passing, outperforming the dataset average of 36.70 and showing your potential. Your strong undergraduate GPA edged slightly toward the risk side compared to averages, while your upper-middle family income and full-time status had smaller influences leaning toward failure risks seen in similar profiles. Overall, compared to typical students who pass, your mid-pack Year 3 grades stood out as the most influential mismatch, even with your strengths elsewhere. This isn't a judgment on your character or effort—it's just how your profile stacked up against historical bar exam outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT-level prep for grades, and reapplying with updated transcripts that demonstrate upward momentum. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, and we're here to support your next steps. Keep pushing—you've got the foundation to turn this around.
